moment_text: Absolutely. So, I mean, I've always had kind of things going on and explained autoimmune, really minor heart condition. And so I was kind of used to going to the doctor and it not being a huge deal. Right? But I had a lump. And even though I knew better, I kind of put it off for a little bit. Um, and I was 39, so pre mammogram age and um, you know, went to the doctor, had their physical exam and then things moved really quickly and literally even through mammogram and ultrasound, I was like, ah, this is gonna be nothing. And then I got my report and my whole world changed. Um, and so. I remember at that moment when the doctors were like, yeah, we're gonna schedule a biopsy before you walk out the door. And I was like, this is serious. You know? And nothing has been the same since.
eligibility_rationale: This moment details the lived experience of delaying care and the subsequent rapid, overwhelming escalation within the healthcare system, highlighting both agency signals (avoidance, seeking care) and strong emotional signals, making it eligible for insight generation.

moment_text: You know, kind of like moments like now I, I vacillate between, I got this and wow, this is wild. You know? And so, um. And then you have the angel on the shoulder and the devil on the shoulder and Hey, this is all great. You got this, and oh, this is awful. And so. It's a constant exercise and balance.

moment_text: I always say, now, looking back, I don't know that I did. It was kinda like, okay, this is what we're doing. Let's do it. You know? I think it was just like, okay, biopsy, lumpectomy. Um, I think the one decision I made was chemo. Um, that was kind of mid, um, score for whether chemo would really be beneficial. And, um, the doctor was kind of like, yeah, let's throw everything at it so hopefully we never have to be here again. Um, and so we did do that, um, radiation. moment_text: I, I did, I, I have an amazing care team and I'm still with them. moment_text: I guess the, just the routine of it all. Knowing what, you know, once, I think the scariest part is not knowing. moment_text: So I'd say first your body, your choice. You get to make the choices that look best for you. Doesn't matter what the next person did or would do. You don't have to follow a certain recipe, you don't have to share loudly or, or get into advocacy, um, unless you choose to. But find your lane and find the tools and the places that are gonna lift you up because that's important and you're gonna need it. And we want to be here to lead, to be leaned on. So, you know, just navigate it. Make your choices for you.
